comments:
Summary
This paper presents MKLDroid, a multi-view, context-aware framework for Android malware detection and fine-grained malicious-code localization. The system constructs multiple program-representation graphs (APIs, permissions, sources/sinks, instruction categories, CFG signatures) from an app’s inter-procedural CFG augmented with reachability context, extracts contextual subgraph features via a Contextual Weisfeiler-Lehman Kernel (CWLK), and combines the resulting kernels using linear Multiple Kernel Learning (SMO-MKL). To enable localization, the authors derive a primal weight vector from the learned dual MKL model and compute per-basic-block, per-method and per-class maliciousness scores (m-scores). The paper reports experiments on large datasets (≈60k apps including wild apps), compares to Drebin, CSBD, and Adagio, and evaluates localization using an augmented Mystique dataset. Results indicate improved F-measure over baselines in several settings (notably in temporally realistic tests) and high recall for localization.

Strengths
- Well-motivated multi-view approach: the paper carefully argues why different PRG abstractions (API dependency, permissions, sources/sinks, instruction categories, CFG signatures) capture complementary aspects of malicious behavior and why contextual information matters. The motivating Geinimi example is clear.
- Methodological contribution: combining a context-aware graph kernel (CWLK) with MKL and deriving an interpretable primal representation for localization is novel in this domain. The mechanism for computing m-scores from the composite primal weight vector is sensible and well explained.
- Large-scale evaluation: experiments use many samples (benchmark + wild), include a history-aware training/test split and a wild-app evaluation, which are realistic and valuable. The localization experiments on repackaged malware variants (MYST extension) and qualitative case studies (ADRD, Geinimi) are useful.
- Honest limitations discussion: the authors acknowledge lack of precise data-flow and dynamic analyses, ICC handling, and online adaptation, and suggest future work.

Weaknesses and points for improvement
- Missing data-flow / inter-component communication: the paper rightly notes that the SRC-SINK abstraction captures control-flow between sources and sinks but not actual data-flow, and that ICCs may be missed. Given that many privacy leaks depend on data-flow and ICC, these omissions reduce robustness and may explain some false positives/negatives; more quantitative discussion of how often ICC or missing data-flow impacted results would help.
- Details on ground truth and labeling: for the wild-app experiment the authors state VirusTotal was used to infer labels but do not specify the threshold or methodology used (e.g., how many AV detections required to label as malware). Clearer disclosure would help assess label noise and its impact on reported metrics.
- Reproducibility details: the paper says m-scores for benchmark apps are released, but it is unclear whether the code (feature extraction, CWLK, MKL pipeline) and trained models/kernels will be released. Releasing code and explicit parameters would greatly aid reproducibility.
- Memory and runtime considerations of the dual->primal conversion: constructing composite vectors for the primal weight vector requires concatenating scaled feature representations of all training samples (or at least the support vectors). For large datasets the memory cost could be substantial; the paper should give more details about memory usage and any practical engineering strategies used (e.g., sparse formats, truncation). Some runtime numbers are given but more breakdown (time spent in SMO-MKL kernel weight optimization vs. kernel feature extraction vs. dual->primal conversion) would help practitioners.
- Adversarial robustness and online adaptation: the manuscript acknowledges these are open problems but could provide preliminary experiments or analysis (e.g., sensitivity to injected benign-looking features, or to simple evasion via adding benign API calls) or at least outline concrete mitigation strategies.
- Localization precision: localization recall is excellent (≈94% in MYST top-10 classes) but precision is low, partly because the ground-truth set has few malicious classes per app. It would be helpful to present additional per-sample examples showing how localization ranks malice among many benign classes, and possibly a metric such as mean average precision (MAP) for localization.

Minor comments
- Some repetition and minor formatting issues in the provided text (likely from conversion) — these should be cleaned in final version.
- It would be helpful to clarify how feature selection (chi-squared to 5000 features for some views) affects interpretability: when features are pruned, are the corresponding contextual subgraphs still meaningfully mappable back to code locations?

Overall assessment and recommendation
This is a solid contribution: it proposes a novel, well-motivated combination of context-aware graph kernels and MKL to achieve multi-view detection and interpretable localization. The experimental evaluation is extensive and realistic, and the paper candidly discusses limitations. I recommend acceptance with minor revisions addressing the points above (clarify VirusTotal labeling, provide more practical details on memory/runtime of the dual->primal step, and improve reproducibility by releasing code and detailed parameters).

Suggested specific revisions
- State the exact VirusTotal labeling policy used for the wild dataset (number of engines, threshold) and, if possible, report sensitivity analyses with alternate thresholds.
- Provide more detail and numbers (memory, time) for the dual->primal conversion and m-score computation, and indicate whether sparse storage was used.
- If possible, release code for feature extraction, CWLK, and the MKL pipeline, or provide detailed pseudo-code and parameter settings to enable reproduction.
- Add (or at least outline) a short evaluation of how ICCs and missing data-flow affect results (even a small sample-based analysis or counts of detected/missed leaks due to ICCs would be informative).

Given its originality, solid evaluation, and practical relevance, I score my recommendation as an 8 out of 10.
